skill.name: accessibility
skill.description: Audit and improve web accessibility following WCAG 2.2 guidelines. Use when asked to 'improve accessibility', 'a11y audit', 'WCAG compliance', 'screen reader support', 'keyboard navigation', or 'make accessible'.
skill.summary: Comprehensive WCAG 2.2 accessibility guidelines for auditing and improving web accessibility
skill.author: addyosmani

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 147 potential issues, but all findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The scanner misidentified markdown documentation patterns (code blocks, WCAG criterion numbers, relative paths) as security vulnerabilities. This is a pure documentation skill with no executable code, network calls, or credential handling.

medium_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positives
medium_findings.description: 88 'external_commands' detections are markdown inline code blocks using backtick syntax (JavaScript/HTML examples). 3 'filesystem' detections are legitimate relative paths between skill documentation files. 14 'sensitive' detections are attribute names like 'aria-labelledby' misinterpreted as file paths. 16 'weak_crypto' detections are WCAG criterion identifiers (e.g., 1.4.3, 1.4.6) misinterpreted as cryptographic algorithms.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: 13 references to external URLs (W3C, deque.com, nvaccess.org) are legitimate documentation links, not security concerns for a documentation-only skill.

content.user_title: Audit and fix accessibility issues with WCAG 2.2
content.value_statement: Web accessibility issues cause users to abandon sites. This skill provides WCAG 2.2 guidelines and code patterns to make content accessible to people with disabilities.
actual_capabilities: Audit HTML for WCAG 2.2 compliance issues
Add proper alt text and ARIA labels to elements
Fix keyboard navigation and focus management
Ensure color contrast meets AA/AAA requirements
Implement accessible form validation and error handling
Create screen reader-friendly dynamic content with live regions
limitations: Cannot test with actual screen readers or assistive technology
Cannot modify third-party services or external APIs
Cannot run automated Lighthouse audits directly
Cannot enforce accessibility beyond code-level recommendations

use_cases.title: Accessibility code review
use_cases.description: Review pull requests for accessibility issues and suggest fixes based on WCAG 2.2 guidelines

use_cases.title: Accessibility audit report
use_cases.description: Generate a prioritized report of accessibility issues with code examples for fixes

use_cases.title: Accessible component implementation
use_cases.description: Create accessible UI components with proper ARIA attributes, keyboard support, and focus management

prompt_templates.title: Quick accessibility check
prompt_templates.prompt: Check this HTML snippet for accessibility issues: [paste HTML here]
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need a fast review of a small code snippet

prompt_templates.title: Full page accessibility audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the following page content for WCAG 2.2 AA compliance and list all issues with severity levels and fix suggestions: [paste HTML content]
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need comprehensive accessibility review

prompt_templates.title: Accessible component implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement an accessible [component type, e.g., modal/dropdown/tabs] following WCAG 2.2 guidelines. Include keyboard navigation, ARIA attributes, and focus management.
prompt_templates.scenario: When building new accessible components from scratch

prompt_templates.title: Fix specific accessibility issue
prompt_templates.prompt: The following component has [specific issue]. Fix it to meet WCAG 2.2 AA requirements: [paste component code]
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to fix known accessibility problems

output_examples.output:
Issue: Button lacks accessible name for screen readers.

Fix: Add aria-label or visible text.
```html
<button aria-label="Close menu">
  <svg aria-hidden="true">icon</svg>
</button>
```

Issue: Input fields lack programmatic labels.

Fix: Add explicit label associations.
```html
<label for="email">Email address</label>
<input type="email" id="email" autocomplete="email">
```

best_practices: Use native HTML elements when possible (button, nav, main) before adding ARIA
Test keyboard navigation by pressing Tab from start to end of the page
Ensure all interactive elements have visible focus indicators
anti_patterns: Using role='button' on div instead of native <button> element
Removing outline:focus styles without providing alternative focus indication
Relying on color alone to convey information without text or icon alternatives

faq.question: What WCAG level should I target?
faq.answer: Target WCAG 2.2 Level AA as the minimum standard. Level AAA is ideal but may not be practical for all content types. Legal requirements in many jurisdictions mandate Level AA.

faq.question: How do I test accessibility without expensive tools?
faq.answer: Use free browser extensions like axe DevTools or WAVE. Test keyboard navigation manually by pressing Tab through your page. Zoom to 200% to check reflow. Use the browser's built-in accessibility inspector.

faq.question: When should I use ARIA attributes?
faq.answer: Use ARIA only when native HTML cannot achieve the needed functionality. Prefer semantic HTML first. When ARIA is needed, follow the First Rule of ARIA: if a native HTML element or attribute provides the same functionality, use it instead.

faq.question: What is the minimum color contrast ratio?
faq.answer: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text and 3:1 for large text (18px+ or 14px+ bold). Enhanced AAA compliance requires 7:1 for normal text and 4.5:1 for large text.

faq.question: How do I make images accessible?
faq.answer: Add descriptive alt text for informative images. Use empty alt (alt='') for decorative images. Provide long descriptions via aria-describedby or figcaption for complex images like charts and infographics.

faq.question: What is the minimum target size for interactive elements?
faq.answer: WCAG 2.2 AA requires interactive targets to be at least 24x24 CSS pixels. The recommended target size is 44x44 pixels for comfortable touch interaction on mobile devices.
